---
title: "Project 1 Grading Rubric"
---


Ideas for Group Collaboration part of rubric:

> A student’s final project grade cannot exceed their contribution grade by
> more than one letter grade.

**A-Level (Excellent Collaborative Practice)**
<!-- - Clear, consistent task ownership across weeks -->
<!-- - Responsibilities rotated meaningfully across projects -->
<!-- - All members contributed substantively to both: -->
<!-- - Their owned task -->
<!-- - Feedback and revision on others’ work -->
<!-- - Work is well-integrated (no siloed sections) -->
<!-- - Contribution surveys consistently indicate balanced workload (mostly 3–4 ratings) -->
<!-- - No unresolved collaboration concerns -->

Demonstrates sustained, equitable collaboration with clear ownership, cross-contribution, and strong integration across all project components.

**B-Level (Strong but Some Imbalance)**

<!-- - Clear ownership structure, but some uneven rotation -->
<!-- - Minor workload imbalance in some weeks -->
<!-- - Collaboration generally effective but occasionally siloed -->
<!-- - Surveys show mostly balanced ratings with isolated concerns -->
<!-- - Issues resolved without formal intervention -->

Demonstrates generally effective collaboration with minor inconsistencies in
workload balance or integration.

**C-Level (Noticeable Collaboration Problems)**

- Inconsistent or unclear ownership
- Evidence of repeated imbalance
- Some members primarily edited rather than substantively contributed
- Surveys show multiple “2” ratings
- Instructor check-in or structured reset required
- Work may show uneven quality across sections

Demonstrates uneven collaboration, inconsistent ownership, or limited 
cross-member contribution.

**D-Level (Serious Collaboration Breakdown)**

- Persistent imbalance despite intervention
- One or more members carried disproportionate workload
- Minimal cross-feedback or integration
- Surveys show repeated “1” ratings
- Documented communication breakdown
- Work appears fragmented or incoherent

Demonstrates sustained imbalance, minimal collaborative engagement, or failure
to meet shared responsibility expectations.
